title: Shinji and his interpretation of what it means to be a man
display_title: Shinji and his interpretation of what it means to be a man
wikitext:
[]: Shinji and his interpretation of what it means to be a man             Quote Post by ChaddyManPrime » Mon May 26, 2014 3:53 am Given what we know of Shinji, what would he think a man should be?

[]:             Quote Post by Stillborn » Mon May 26, 2014 4:27 am With how much Anno is showing that he won't let Shinji have a pair? Well with Shinji's self esteem, thats pretty much everything that has testicles and is not him. Even Kaworu would be manly by Shinji's standards.

[]:             Quote Post by pwhodges » Mon May 26, 2014 4:45 am I can see this topic bringing out the worst in the forum...

[]:             Quote Post by robersora » Mon May 26, 2014 5:54 am pwhodges wrote: I can see this topic bringing out the worst in the forum...

[]:             Quote Post by Stillborn » Mon May 26, 2014 5:59 am Oh yes, personal insults are such a great way to show how much more you contribute to this thread than me No one denies Kaworu's maturity and often inhuman insight. But for probably no one else but Shinji Kaworu could be called manly.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Mon May 26, 2014 7:16 am No preemptive thread-crapping or personal attacks, please. A few of you could stand to refresh your understanding of rules sec. 2.5

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Mon May 26, 2014 8:56 am I don't think he's particularly sure. If you ask him, he'd probably say, "probably like Touji or Kaji-san", but he didn't have much orientation in his life before, given his father's generally distant presence. Shini didn's see him at all in the three years before the series, so he would've been a prepubescent child the last time they met. He sure tries to assert himself as a dude throughout the series, somewhat clumsily at times (See ep 16 - I don't think he actually believes Asuka and Rei are less fit for battle than he is, but, "I man, I killer!" is just a commonly known standard way that is used for the purpose of making yourself as a proud XY person for those who are too old to resort to light blue and GI Joes. It ultimately IS a social construct, although people do have an inbuilt drive to be recognized as what their hypothalamus tells them they are. It doesn't help that this is an emergent trait, ie, a baby will already carry certain predispositions (typically towards being a heterosexual case of the gender that matches their junk), but they won't reveal themselves untill the kid is 3/4 or in puberty and builds a certain understanding of themselves. Telling the world wether they are a girl or a boy is typically very serious business for your average toddler, but because they still have the simplistic understanding of small children, they resort to simplistic things like princess costumes. They look to society for how to do this marking. In Shinji's case, there was this person demanding stereotypica behavior of him (Asuka), and in that situation, he felt confident enough to say, "Well, you can have it if you want it so badly!"/snap back at her instead of just taking her abuse. ) Basically, he hears that it's expected of him, but he isn't quite sure how to do it (See also the ep 18 conversation with Kaji) --- We should really see "man" as a counterpoint/opposite to"little boy" rather than of "woman". That just mkes ppl turn the opposite sex into a 'container' (the phychological terms) for all they don't want to be or aren't allowed to be, which creates problems when you want to interact with them as people.

[]:             Quote Post by robersora » Mon May 26, 2014 9:13 am Stillborn wrote: Oh yes, personal insults are such a great way to show how much more you contribute to this thread than me

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Mon May 26, 2014 10:04 am robersora wrote: Maybe that's where his excessive "I mustn't run away" comes from, because he feels that his father ran away from his obligation to raise his son.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Mon May 26, 2014 11:39 am Gender roles are damaging to individualism and perpetuates the battle of the sexes; Eva is aware of this. I think a show concerned with teaching the characters to love themselves as they are is less concerned with trying to make its "wimpy protagonist" into a more traditional interpretation of what it means to be "masculine". So, instead of asking what Shinji's preconceived notions of what himself as a "man" would be, why don't we ask why Shinji's preconceived notions of what himself as a mature, functioning adult would be? Shinji has self-esteem issues, and since he has no real parental figures in his life he has had very few people to look up to and base his worldview on when growing up; Shinji's dilemma is due that fact he never really learned how to live with other people. He simply didn't know what to do, so he did as he was told; he took a passive role in his own life before we meet him in Ep 01 of the show. The show itself is about learning to taking action in ones own life and learning to handle the consequences of those actions. So, Shinji's idea of a man (or really, just simply a human ) should probably be somebody that is confident in themselves and is able to take action when necessary; somebody that can make the right decision, but also handle the fallout from making an occasional bad decision; and somebody that can accepts themselves for who they are, even if they don't fit into a specific mold, such as masculinity. Kaji plays a kind of pseudo-father figure role for Shinji, and I think he would be a good role model to look up to. Kaji is no stranger to the hardships of having a rough childhood, yet he chooses to keep his head up and enjoy life always smiling, even when staring death in the face. At the same time, he can also get deathly serious when a situation calls for it. He's also got healthy hobbies, and isn't absolutely obsessed with his work. Overall, I think Kaji is probably one of the most interesting characters, because he is one of the only all-around adjusted adult characters, and he has intense personal convictions that he follows even to his grave; he can make a decision and accept the consequences of his actions. He also shows that he lives for the greater good of humanity, and not just for himself, which is commendable. Shinji should try to be more like him, and less like Gendo.

[]:             Quote Post by EvangelionGodMode » Mon May 26, 2014 10:28 pm Im just gonna try to put it in layman's term,because im just a layman. Judging by the information the series gives us,Shinji has only had a few adult males in his life. The one he probably respects the most even if he won't admit is his dad. Seeing how Gendo is always cool and collective, Shinji thinks that Gendo is very brave and has few fears. And Shinji sees this and probably figures that since he will never have the courage and strength that Gendo has, his father will never be impressed by him and love him. So Shinji basically thinks to become a man, he probably will need to become strong enough to impress his father.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Tue May 27, 2014 12:55 am Hm. I think Gendo is pretty much an opaque force of nature to Shinji, not something he's ascribe motivations to with any degree of certainty.

[]:             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Tue May 27, 2014 3:15 am ^ Shinji does admit that his main reason for piloting is to receive the praise (and with it the acceptance) of his father, at least until Bardiel. So he probably thinks that he must be brave and showing as little fear as possible to be like Gendo and be accepted by him, that belief was probably reinforced when Shinji was praised by Gendo at the end of the Sahaquiel battle, where he showed a great cool and bravery (even more so in Rebuild where it was good enough to impress Asuka and have her have an epiphany about her worldview) After Bardiel, I think that for him "be a man" (more like "be an adult" honestly) would be to be someone like Kaji, who imparted some life lessons, notably about finding his own reasons to pilot during Zeruel's attack, which led Shinji to shift his reasons for piloting from "being praised by Gendo" to "protect those I care for". The title of that episode is even "A man's battle".

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Tue May 27, 2014 6:23 am ElMariachi wrote: So he probably thinks that he must be brave and showing as little fear as possible to be like Gendo and be accepted by him,

[]:             Quote Post by Sgt. Griff » Wed May 28, 2014 12:28 am The nature of Masculinity at the moment, much like in Shinji's mind, is in question. It's place, forms and definition continue to change, and putting a finger on the pulse of this term only serves to stop the flow of blood. Speaking as a boy more or less of Shinji's age, more or less in the same year, I can say that the way the many somewhat vague values of masculinity are learned is through watching other males. That is to say, Shinji's view of what masculinity means would change between episodes, much like in real life. Certainly the influence of his father and Kaworu are the greatest on these views, however, with the end of ep 24, it would probably be safe to say he'd learned the responsibilities, kindness, and sacrifices it entails. Had he chosen to accept this, ep 25' and 26' would have played out rather differently. God, I have no idea what I'm talking about.

[]:             Quote Post by ChaddyManPrime » Wed May 28, 2014 12:32 am ^ Hey bro, at least you are trying, that's what's important.

[]:             Quote Post by LeoXiao » Sun Jun 01, 2014 9:12 pm I think Shinji acts the way he's raised: to do his work obediently and quietly. Of course he laughs at jokes made by the likes of Touji and Kensuke, listens to the advice of people like Kaji, and fantasizes about having a different relationship with the girls and women in his life, but that all belongs to the realm of the unreal, stuff that doesn't happen in reality. When something like the kissing scene with Asuka happens, even though it's something Shinji likely dreams about all the time, he gets scared and has no idea how to handle it because it falls outside of his conception of "plausible real life event". So what does this mean for Shinji's idea of manliness? Well you could say that he doesn't actually know what it means to be a man, which is true in the sense that he's only 14, but I think that's too simple. He probably wants to be something like Kaji, or worse, his dad, but doesn't see himself as being that person at all. So he just sticks to his reliable, tested method of doing his job the way he thinks people expect of him. Of course, since his job happens to be PILOTING A GIANT ROBOT THAT DOESN'T WORK HALF THE TIME, and to live in the company of a bunch of girls who don't just give and take orders, this method of his starts losing its validity in the course of the show. At the same time, there's nothing that replaces his prior conception, or that at least augments it to be less stiff. If this were a classical Bildungsroman, we'd probably have someone like Kaji fill in as Shinji's male role model in lieu of his dad, but the problem is that Shinji doesn't live with Kaji, and Kaji only talks with him a couple times before being killed. Not to mention that Shinji is, ultimately, a vastly different person from Kaji and with an even more different situation, so even having that role model may not have helped if Shinji due to his unique situation of being a pilot. Kaji knew this, so that's why he said something like "I can only stand here and water plants, but you have something that only you can do. Wanna do it?" Basically, the only hope for change is to change yourself. But, what is to be done when you don't know how to change yourself? The answer seems, weirdly, to be "God", at least in the sense that Shinji's headspace and conversations with Unit 01, Rei, and the other "divine" actors in the show serves as a confession chamber. In the end, it seems to be just such a "confession process" brought about by Instrumentality and Unit 01 that allows Shinji to reflect on himself and reach his conclusion about the conditions for being human and leading a human existence.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Mon Jun 02, 2014 11:41 am LeoXiao wrote: When something like the kissing scene with Asuka happens, even though it's something Shinji likely dreams about all the time, he gets scared and has no idea how to handle it because it falls outside of his conception of "plausible real life event".

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Mon Jun 02, 2014 1:03 pm Kendrix wrote: That's a pretty oversimplified reading of that scene.

[]:             Quote Post by James Sucellus » Mon Jun 02, 2014 4:13 pm He hates himself for being a coward, which must surely tie in with the vox populi's definition of how a 'masculine' man should behave, which Shinji feels he fails to live up to.
